Multicorner Timing Analysis Acceleration  
for Iterative Physical Design of ICs  
Wei W. Xing , Member, IEEE, Longze Wang, Student Member, IEEE, Zhelong Wang, Student Member, IEEE,  
Zhaoyu Shi, Student Member, IEEE, Ning Xu, Member, IEEE, Yuanqing Cheng , Senior Member, IEEE,  
and Weisheng Zhao , Fellow, IEEE  
Abstract—We propose a multicorner multistage timing analysis below [5]. The explosion of process corner count presents a  
prediction framework using a generalized linear model with  
latent features. We then further improve such methods using  
kernel trick extension, transfer learning with knowledge from  
previous designs, and multioutput feature engineering to deliver  
state-of-the-art (SOTA) prediction accuracy with very limited  
challenge for traditional STA—extremely high-computational  
cost. Even worse, the conventional physical design goes  
through synthesis, ﬂoorplan, power plan, placement, clock  
synthesis, routing, and engineering change order (ECO) opti-  
training data. Most importantly, our method is equipped with mizations. Each stage requires extensive timing simulations to  
a Bayesian decision strategy to deliver reliable predictions  
with accuracy close to 100%, pushing the frontier of the  
machine-learning-based static timing analysis (STA) for practical  
implementation in the industry environment, where reliability is  
highly desired. Experimental results show that the accuracy of  
pinpoint the timing failure as early as possible. Therefore, STA  
has become an ever-increasing time-consuming procedure in  
modern chip timing veriﬁcation.  
To reduce the timing analysis overhead, several effective  
our proposed method outperforms the SOTA competitors by up techniques have been proposed, which can be mainly clas-  
to 4x and can improve prediction accuracy to 100% with little  
extra STA executions.  
siﬁed into two categories. One is to reduce the time to that  
of one process corner analysis [6], [7]. More speciﬁcally,  
Silva et al. [6] used a branch-and-bound method to obtain the  
worst-case process corner for chip sign-off timing analysis.  
Based on a similar idea, Onaissi and Najm [7] derived the  
worst-case timing results much faster than a normal multi-  
corner STA. By estimating the worst-case timing among PCs,  
the upper bound of the worst timing of the critical path can  
be evaluated with one-pass analysis instead of running STA  
for all process corners. However, the upper bound may not  
be a strictly tight bound, leading to an overdesign with a  
conservatively large design margin. Moreover, the worst case  
may not occur in practical operational conditions because the  
combinations of PVT leading to the worst-case delay are less  
likely to occur simultaneously. Another category of accelerat-  
ing STA is taking the process and environment parameters as  
independent random variables, and using statistical techniques  
for one-pass timing analysis, such as [8], [9].  
Index Terms—Active learning, Bayesian decision theory,  
featured engineering, Gaussian process, machine learning, mul-  
tiprocess corners, static timing analysis (STA).  
I. INTRODUCTION  
N ORDER to guarantee the timing yield, chip designers  
resort to static timing analysis (STA) to evaluate the timing  
I
correctness under different process corners (PCs) [1]. Thanks  
to the fast speed and reliability of STA, it has been widely  
used for timing veriﬁcation in the past few decades. As the  
technology node scales down into the nanometer regime, the  
process, voltage, and temperature (PVT) variations impact  
chip performance signiﬁcantly [2]. As a result, the number of  
process corners increases drastically with the technology node  
scaling [3], [4]. For instance, the number of process corners  
rockets up to over 100 in the 20-nm technology node and  
Recently, inspired by the success of machine learning in  
EDA ﬁelds, researchers and industry have started to investigate  
the use of data-driven techniques to accelerate STA. Andrew

To resolve these issues, we solve the STA prediction  
problem around all main physical design stages in a unique  
space taking advantage of their close correlations. We propose  
a multilinear regression model with implicit latent features to  
harness the historical information and predict the STA results  
accurately. We then generalize our model as a nonlinear model  
to deal with complex STA problems along with a scalable and  
efﬁcient model training procedure. Besides providing more  
accurate prediction than traditional methods, the proposed  
method provides a Bayesian decision strategy based on the  
predictive distribution to actively update the model for uncer-  
tain predictions and improve the prediction accuracy to nearly  
100% with little cost. This way, the timing predictions are  
given with conﬁdence, and the results are reliable, making the  
proposed method more practical to be implemented in a real  
industrial design. Furthermore, feature engineering is added to  
our method to further improve the prediction accuracy.  
In summary, our contributions are listed as follows.  
1) We propose a novel ﬂexible framework for the STA  
prediction problem with latent features that enable  
knowledge transfer from previous experimental data.  
2) We establish a Bayesian nonlinear model that is accu-  
rate, scalable, and ﬂexible for timing prediction.  
B. Classic STA Prediction Methods  
Ideally, we would like to directly predict the STA matrix  
Y(z) using a machine learning model for any given circuit z.  
However, this is almost an impossible task because we cannot  
quantify z, i, nor j as model inputs, not to mention their  
complex interactions. To resolve this challenge, [10], [11]  
compromise the ultimate goal of directly predicting Y(z).  
Instead, they make it a matrix inﬁll task, where the STA  
matrix Y(z) is partially observed, and the goal is to predict the  
missing entries. Denote the ith column of Y(z) as y(z, i). Their  
method ﬁrst decides a set of Iꢁ (Iꢁ << I) PCs that matter most  
in the predictions and uses this as model input for a regressioꢂn  
model. The inputs are then x = (y(z, 1, j), . . . , y(z, Iꢁ, j))  
whereas the model target output are deﬁned as t = (y(z, Iꢁ +  
1, j), . . . , y(z, I, j))ꢂ. This way, each row of Y(z) in sampling  
data D produces a model input and corresponding output pair  
to train a regression model. Note that the selection of the  
corner can be arbitrary without following an ordinal order.  
The goal is to approximate the mapping from the known STA  
results x to the unknown results t with existing data  
t = g(x)  
(1)  
3) We put forth multioutput feature engineering (MoFe) to  
further improve model accuracy.  
where g represents the mapping, which can be a linear or  
nonlinear regression model. Due to the fact that x and t are  
highly correlated, Zhao et al. [11] showed the efﬁciency of  
such an approach in successfully predicting y, which reduces  
the total number of STA simulations by a ratio of 1 − (Iꢁ/I).  
Despite its novelty, this method has several limitations.  
First, the model depends on preselected PCs, which do not  
generalize well to different circuits. Because the model is  
trained with a ﬁxed set of PCs, it cannot adapt to different  
circuits by using other PCs as inputs.  
4) We proposed a Bayesian decision strategy to assist STA,  
enhancing the reliability of STA predictions with active  
correction such that the model accuracy is almost 100%,  
making the proposed method, more practical for real  
chip designs.  
The remainder of this article is organized as follows.  
Section II formulates the STA prediction problem and analyzes  
the drawbacks of the classic approach, which motivates this  
work. Section III describes the proposed method with detailed  
key techniques involved. Section IV illustrates the experiment  
results and Section V concludes our work.  
Second, the predictions lack uncertainty quantiﬁcation.  
When it makes predictions, it is hard to tell how reliable the  
predictions are. This hinders it from being used in a real STA  
ﬂow where wrong predictions can lead to a catastrophe.  
II. PROBLEM FORMULATION AND PRELIMINARIES  
A. Problem Formulation

B. Nonlinear Regression Generalization  
Equation (3) is a linear regression model, which is simple  
and efﬁcient to train. However, it is not ﬂexible enough to  
capture the complex inﬂuence of the process corners and the  
critical paths to the timing results. To address this issue and  
deal with practical challenging STA predictions, we extend the  
linear regression model to a nonlinear regression model.  
First, we introduce a feature mapping function φ(·),  
which maps the original input features z, ci, pj to a higher-  
dimensional feature space φ(z, ci, pj). If we choose such a  
mapping appropriately, we can capture the complex inﬂuence  
of the process corners and the critical paths to the timing  
results. The model becomes  
A. Linear Regression Model With Latent Features  
We ﬁrst put forth a multilinear regression model, which is  
ﬂexible and efﬁcient to enhance the existing classic approach  
for timing prediction. The original goal of predicting Y(z) is  
broken down into predicting its components y(z, i, j) one by  
one as an equivalent goal. We can ﬁrst introduce a simple  
linear regression model

where Skk is the kth diagonal element of S. With the previous

only takes O(NIJ) time complexity. For implementation, the

computation with space complexity of O(I2 +J2 +N2), which  
is also implemented in this work.  
This way, we reproduce a Gaussian process regression  
(GPR [15]) model tailored for our STA prediction problem.  
Note that the latent features ci, pj and the hyperparameters ꢁ  
and θ0 are to be determined.

observations of data D, we can write the joint distribution as

F. Multiobjective Feature Engineering

∗

F
ˆ
To further enhance the performance of the timing prediction  
method, an effective way is to introduce automatic feature  
engineering, which generates high-dimensional features xˆ to  
replace the original features x as model inputs and improve the  
predictive accuracy with a signiﬁcant margin. This approach  
remains effective even when we construct the mapping. using  
kernel methods as in GP. Despite that there are many existing  
automatic feature engineering approaches, e.g., Deep feature  
synthesis [22], Tsfresh [23], and AutoFeat [24], none of  
the state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods generalize well to our  
problem, which requires predictions under several corners. Our  
task is essentially a multiobjective automatic feature engi-  
neering problem, which is not well resolved in the automatic  
feature engineering literature. The task is nontrivial because  
the multiple objective data are correlated but different. This  
makes it difﬁcult to measure the importance of a feature  
(which is an essential component of feature engineering), i.e.,  
a feature can improve accuracy under corner A but degrade  
accuracy under corner B.  
predict Y.  
Such a point estimation might not be robust to the noise  
in Y. we can use a Laplace approximation [19] or variational

This integration is usually intractable and can be approximated  
by Monte Carlo sampling, which is a computationally expen-  
sive task despite of its elegance. For an efﬁcient alternative  
solution to approximate the integral, we follow [20] to make  
predictions using a GP for Y with Y as inputs. Such a GP  
model can be trained quickly with existing data D using the  
similar approach method in (1). Despite its simplicity, this  
approach works very well in our experiments.  
ˆ
ˇ
E. Conﬁdence Evaluation and Active Correction  
To solve this challenge, we propose multiobjective feature  
engineering (MoFe) to enable general automatic feature engi-  
neering for our problem. Denote the target (training) output  
Y ∈ RN×D corresponding to inputs X here. Our method ﬁrst  
ﬁnds its linear representations, which are ideally independent,

15: return Features and importance {Xꢁr, er}r=1

of candidate features. In order to improve the practicality, we  
introduce a successive halving approach. We divide the total  
samples into 2J blocks, each with training and validation set at  
a ratio of 9:1. We incrementally expand the data size from one  
block to all blocks, which is used to rank the candidate features  
and remove the ineffective ones by half in each iteration. This  
stage aims at rough screening, where we assess the incremental  
effect of individual new features. Therefore, our method runs  
for all blocks until 2j.  
The limitation with the above approach is that the candidate  
features are based on the existing features; the higher-order  
features (features generated based on generated features) are  
not considered. To address this issue, we repeat the previous  
process K times, and each time we use the features generated  
in the previous iteration as the existing features to generate  
new features.  
The process is repeated for each column of U to get features  
and their importance scores {Xꢁr, er}. The importance scores  
to the multioutput Y is then calculated by er · sr, where sr is  
the rth column of S and the corresponding eigenvalue of ur.  
This is the complete process of the MoFe algorithm which is  
summarized in Algorithm 2. Base on the result, we can select  
the top n features with the highest-importance scores and add  
them to the input to our model. Although our model only  
used LightGBM in the feature selection stage and did not use  
it for actual prediction, we will show in the experiment that  
the features selected by MoFe can signiﬁcantly improve the  
performance of the model.  
statistically independent of each other. Thus, we can apply  
automatic feature engineering to each coefﬁcient individually.  
The key ingredient of MoFe is to apply automatic feature  
engineering to each coefﬁcient individually ﬁrst and compare  
their importance later based on the feature signiﬁcance and  
the principal direction variance.  
We now discuss the feature engineering part in detail  
inspired by OpenFE [26], which use an expand-reduce frame-  
work to ﬁrst generate (expand) as many possible candidate  
features and remove the less relevant candidate features. First  
we design a feature engineering function φ that enumerates  
all ﬁrst-order transformations of the basic features using given  
operators to create a huge candidate feature pool. Appendix  
contains the list of operators and how we use them to transform  
numerical features. For instance, if the basic features are  
x(1) and x(2), the candidate feature pool becomes {x(1)  
+
x(2), x(1) − x(2), x(1) ∗ x(2), x(1)/x(2), x(2)/x(1), (x(1))2, (x(2))2}  
given the operator set {+, −, ∗, /, x2}.  
Generating the feature pool is fast and straightforward. The  
challenge is to keep only the most important features that  
generate the best-predictive accuracy. To this end, we use  
a gradient-boosting-like method to test how much a given  
feature can improve the predictive accuracy and determine its  
importance. More speciﬁcally, we ﬁrst train a LightGBM M0  
on data {X, ur} using ﬁvefold cross-validation, where ur is  
the rth column of U. Based on M0, we test all features in  
the candidate feature pool individually and concludes their  
importance based on the accuracy improvement (computed on  
the validation set). Since the model is trained on M0 with  
only a new feature each time, this approach is fast and can  
be applied to a large number of candidate features. We call  
this Feature Ranking, which is a key component of MoFe

We evaluate the performance of the proposed technique

TABLE II  
PARAMETERS OF FREEPDK 45-NM TECHNOLOGY PROCESS CORNERS  
TABLE III  
To stay consistent with the previous work [11], we use mean  
absolute error (MAE) and LESS10, which represents the  
prediction error is less than 10% times of the true value.  
In each iteration, we require a certain amount of time to  
complete the timing analysis under PCs. Since the required  
time in each iteration does not vary signiﬁcantly, we take the  
average of the STA analysis time required in ﬁve physical  
design stages of b17 as the “run time of prediction” for  
generating timing-critical path slack using traditional STA  
tools, as shown in Table IV. We use the average of the times  
predicted by our model as the STA prediction run time of  
our method. Here, “1 corner/3 corners/5 corners” represents  
using timing analysis results from 1/3/5 known corners to  
predict timings for the remaining unknown process corners.  
The term “Training time” indicates the time required by  
different models during the training process. Similarly, we  
calculate the average prediction times for RL, MLP, and RF.  
Although the proposed method takes slightly longer time, our  
accuracy is far higher than other methods, and it can also  
provide conﬁdence evaluations.  
IWLS BENCHMARKS USED IN OUR EXPERIMENTS  
We thus adopt the more sophisticated RR as an alternative as  
a representation of the linear regression-based method. Our  
method is implemented based on modern machine learning  
library PyTorch [27] whereas the other baseline methods are  
implemented on scikit library [28] as suggested in [11].  
To prepare the evaluation data, we use the publicly available  
FreePDK 45-nm technology and Nangate 45-nm standard  
cell library to generate design data for model training and  
validation. The process corners included in the standard cell  
library are used in our following experiments. In order to  
mimic the multicorner cases in real deep submicron design  
environment, we generated 14 process corners based on the  
limited process corners provided in the PDK. The process  
corner parameters of 45-nm technology nodes are listed in

C. STA Prediction With Timing Optimization Iterations  
In practical timing optimizations, designers normally iter-  
atively alter a design to approach timing closure. This  
process generates similar STA-matrices, which can be used  
to enhance the proposed technique’s capability by knowl-  
edge transferred from previous timing optimization iterations.  
Furthermore, our model has the prediction capability with  
Authorized licensed use limited to: Thapar Institute of Engineering & Technology. Downloaded on March 23,2026 at 16:50:36 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.  
   
XING et al.: MULTICORNER TIMING ANALYSIS ACCELERATION FOR ITERATIVE PHYSICAL DESIGN OF ICs  
2159  
TABLE VI  
LESS10 AND MAE IN THE ABSENCE OF A TRAINING  
SET FOR PREDICTING MATRICES  
the prediction accuracy gradually increases with the number  
of iterations, indicating that our model can effectively utilized  
the results from the timing results. This is particularly useful  
in the timing optimization procedure, where the optimization  
process can now be accelerated by using the timing results  
of previous iterations to predict the timing results of the next  
iteration. It is also noticeable that the prediction accuracy of  
b19 suddenly drops at the 3rd iteration, which is possibly  
because the circuit undergoes a signiﬁcant change in the 3rd  
iteration, which makes the previous timing results less useful.  
Nevertheless, the performance quickly improves in the next  
iteration, which shows the robustness of our model.  
Furthermore, if we do not utilize partial information from  
the target timing results, while using another previous fully  
known timing matrices as the training set, we would still  
obtain a similar prediction accuracy, with an average LESS10  
of 70.46% in the case of b17 with three dominant corners. As  
shown in Table VI, in this scenario, our method’s accuracy  
is still higher than other methods. However, by incorporating  
partial known information from the target timing results, along  
with previous timing results, the prediction accuracy can be  
signiﬁcantly improved.

testing ratio is 0.7 − 0.3 in b17, b18, and b19.

D. Reliable Evaluations With Active Correction  
To further evaluate the reliability of our proposed technique,  
we ﬁrst examine the reliability of the predictive posterior dis-  
tribution of our model by comparing the predicted conﬁdence  
interval with the true value. Particularly, we will compare  
the truth value with the predicted mean and variance of the  
Gaussian distribution to see if the truth value is within the 95%  
conﬁdence interval of the predicted value. If that is the case,  
it means that the predicted posterior distribution is reliable.  
only timing results of a part of observed critical timing  
paths to predict the rest of the critical path timing. More

benchmarks with 45-nm technology node.  
However, the 97.1% proportion within 2σ accuracy is  
probably not enough to guarantee the reliability in practical  
applications. To further demonstrate the advantage of having a  
predictive posterior as discussed in Section III-E, we conduct  
the conﬁdence evaluation with the active correction process  
discussed in Section III-E to run STA for the paths with high  
possibility of being crucially wrong, i.e., those paths with  
a large chance to fail the STA. This process will increase  
the computational cost but guarantee the reliability of the  
prediction. We deﬁne three key indicators to examine the  
performance: 1) Coverage, the ratio of the wrong predictions  
correctly marked divided by the total number of wrong  
predictions (higher is better); 2) ExcessRatio, the total number  
of correctly and incorrectly marked paths divided by the num-  
ber of wrong predictions (lower is better); and 3) ExtraCost,  
the total number of marked paths, which need rerun of STA for  
these points, divided by the initial STA runs (lower is better).  
We conduct this active correction process on the b17,  
b18, b19 benchmarks with 45-nm technology node and three  
corners and ﬁve corners. The results are shown in Fig. 7,  
where the coverage, excess ratio, and extra cost are shown.  
Fig. 7. Key indicators of {3, 5} corners (from left to right) in b17, b18, and  
b19 (from top to bottom).  
of corners increases, the extra cost decreases signiﬁcantly.  
This demonstrates that with an increasing number of known  
corners, we can achieve more reliable corrections with fewer  
costs for all cases.

improve our model despite that our method is not based

of our method with and without feature engineering for

technique to deal with iterative timing optimizations with a

real circuit design with advanced technology node.  
APPENDIX  
OPERATORS AND FEATURE TRANSFORMATIONS  
We enumerate all the ﬁrst-order transformations to form the  
candidate feature set. A ﬁrst-order transformation uses one  
operator once to transform base features.  
We list the details of all the operators below. f stands for a  
numerical feature.  
1) Freq(f): The frequency of feature f.  
2) Abs(f): Element-wise absolute value.  
3) Log(f): Element-wise logarithm.  
4) Sqrt(f): Element-wise square root.  
5) Sigmoid(f): Element-wisely apply function x  
→
(1/1 + e−x).  
6) Round(f): Element-wise rounding.  
7) Residual(f): Element-wisely take decimal part.  
8) Min(f1; f2): Element-wise minimum.  
9) Max(f1; f2): Element-wise maximum.  
10) f1 + f2: Element-wise addition.  
11) f1 − f2: Element-wise subtraction.  
12) f1 × f2: Element-wise multiplication.  
13) f1 ÷ f2: Element-wise division.

without feature engineering.  
2
[2] A. K. M. M. Islam and H. Onodera, “pvt : Process, voltage, temperature  
and time-dependent variability in scaled CMOS process,” in Proc. Int.  
Conf. Comput.-Aided Design, 2018, pp. 1–6.  
[3] J. Hu, D. Sinha, and I. Keller, “Tau 2014 contest on removing common  
path pessimism during timing analysis,” in Proc. Int. Symp. Phys.  
Design, 2014, pp. 591–591.  
of using feature engineering, and the average improvement  
is 4.7% over all design cases. Our work can be replicated  
by using the same dataset and training methodology, which  
will result in obtaining the same features automatically. To  
better showcase the features we generated, we list the features  
generated in the timing prediction of b17. We have speciﬁed  
the selection of the top 20 features: (l2 + l3), abs(l3), (l1/l2),  
residual(l2), max(l1, l2), (l1/l3), min(l1, l2), (l2 − l3), (l1 −l3),  
(l1 + l2), (l1 + l3), abs(l1), (l1 − l2), (l1 ∗ l3), (l1 ∗ l2), (l2 ∗ l3),  
residual(l3), residual(l1), freq(l1), and min(l1, l3), where l1  
represents the ﬁrst column, l2 represents the second column,  
and l3 represents the third column. The deﬁnitions of the  
operators are in the Appendix.

timing analysis: Incremental timing and CPPR analysis,” in Proc.  
IEEE/ACM Int. Conf. Comput.-Aided Design, 2015, pp. 882–889.

how-16nm-and-14nm-FinFETs-require-new-spice-simulators/.

of the worst-delay corner,” in Proc. Design, Autom. Test Eur. Conf.

analysis covering all process corners,” IEEE Trans. Comput.-Aided

for faster timing closure,” Electronics, vol. 11, no. 10, p. 1571, 2022.  
[12] K-J. Chang, L.-F. Chang, R. G. Mathews, and M. G. Walker, “Method  
and system for extraction of parasitic interconnect impedance including  
inductance,” U.S. Patent, 6 643 831, Nov. 4, 2003.  
We propose a novel nonlinear machine learning-based  
timing prediction model. The model takes latent features of  
process corners, path timings and circuit information in dif-  
ferent timing optimization iterations to improve the prediction  
accuracy. Then, the model is equipped with an active cor-  
rection and feature engineering to obtain higher-prediction  
accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate the model’s supe-  
riority in terms of accuracy and scalability compared to the  
SOTA work. The future work includes improving the proposed

robust startup condition over PVT variations and enhanced oscillation  
swing,” in Proc. (ESSCIRC), 2011, pp. 499–502.

Zhelong Wang (Student Member, IEEE) received  
the B.S. degree in electronic science and tech-  
nology engineering from the Wuhan University of  
Technology, Wuhan, China, in 2022, where he is

science and technology engineering.  
His research focuses on static timing analysis and  
digital integrated circuit backend design.

Ning Xu (Member, IEEE) received the Ph.D.  
degree in electronic science and technology from the  
University of Electronic Science and Technology of  
China, Chengdu, China, in 2003.  
He was then a Postdoctoral Fellow with Tsinghua  
University, Beijing, China, from 2003 to 2005. He is  
currently a Professor with the School of Information  
Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology,  
Wuhan, China. His research interests include EDA,  
image processing, and artiﬁcial intelligence.

Yuanqing Cheng (Senior Member, IEEE) received  
the Ph.D. degree from the Institute of Computing  
Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing,  
China, in 2012.  
He is currently a Tenured Associate Professor  
with Beihang University, Beijing. He has published  
more than 50 peer review papers including  
Proceedings of the IEEE, DAC, ICCAD, IEEE  
TRANSACTIONS ON VERY LARGE SCALE  
INTEGRATION, and IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON  
COMPUTER-AIDED DESIGN OF INTEGRATED  
CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS. His research interests include reliable and physical  
design for 3-D integrated circuits, reliable, and low-power design for emerging  
technologies, such as spintronics and carbon nanotube technologies.  
Wei W. Xing (Member, IEEE) received the Ph.D.  
degree in engineering from the University of  
Warwick, Coventry, U.K., in 2017.  
He was a Postdoctoral Fellow with the Scientiﬁc  
Computing Institute, The University of Utah, Salt  
Lake City, UT, USA, until 2020. He is the author  
of more than 45 peer-reviewed papers includ-  
ing DAC, ASP-DAC, NeurIPS, AAAI, Journal  
of Cybersecurity and Privacy, Journal of Positive  
Sciences, and EST. He is currently an Assistant  
Professor with Beihang University, Beijing, China.  
His research interest is mainly in reliable machine learning for EDA and  
scientiﬁc computing, AI4science, and digital twins.  
Weisheng Zhao (Fellow, IEEE) received the Ph.D.  
degree in physics from the University of Paris Sud,  
Paris, France, in 2007.  
In 2009, he was a Tenured Research Scientist with  
French National Research Center. Since 2014, he  
has been a Distinguished Professor with Beihang  
University, Beijing, China, where he is currently  
a Professor with the School of Integrated Circuit  
Science and Engineering. He has published more  
than 200 scientiﬁc articles in leading journals and  
conferences, such as Nature Electronics, Nature  
Longze Wang (Student Member, IEEE) received  
the B.S. degree from Beihang University, Beijing,  
China, in 2013, where he is currently pursuing the  
M.S. degree in electrical engineering.  
His research interests include application of  
machine learning in static timing analysis.  
Communications, Advanced Materials, IEEE TRANSACTIONS, ISCA, and  
DAC. His current research interests include the hybrid integration of nanode-  
vices with CMOS circuit and new nonvolatile memory (40-nm technology  
node and below), such as MRAM circuit and architecture design.
